sentences_input.text: We finally had to sell it to the Nazi party for 100 marks.
The 100 marks we never got, either.
This is a picture of Hella and myself, in-- on a bench in Central Park.
We had just arrived from England and had no idea what our fate would be.
This is a picture of our oldest son Ralph Hammelbacher.
He stayed in Kenya for 10 years, a good part of the time as a safari guide, then he went into the-- into the travel wholesale business.
And this was when he was flying in Kenya in pursuit of various routes in Kenya so he could have his customers come over to Kenya and get around.
That must have been taken around-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] around-- around 1970.
Wasn't it October?
This is a wedding good picture of son, Frank, with Janet, nee Ingber, in September of '93, in New York.
This is my passport issued in Nuremberg in 1938, by mistake.
Without that passport, I don't know if I would ever have gotten out of Germany alive.
This is the first page of my passport showing the red J for Jude, Jew, that every German Jew had.
And every man was given the second name of Israel.
So I'm now Dr.
Hans Israel Hammelbacher.
The women were all called Sarah, in addition to their regular first name.

This is a calendar that Hella and I bring out every year, on behalf of the Blue Card, a charitable organization that takes care of Holocaust survivors in this country by making it possible for them to pay their rent and deductibles and grocery bills.
We have various themes that we think of.

This is the second in a serious called 'The Jewish Pioneers in America,' showing a picture of the statue which stands on Wacker Drive in Chicago showing George Washington in the center; the Secretary of the Treasury, Morris, on the left; and Haym Salomon on the right, who financed the revolution.
Without Haym Salomon, there would have been no money to pay for the troops that fought for the United States.
